  • After outer_func returns, we print the global , which is now 1. This shows the difference: in inner_func did not touch outer_func’s local . If we wanted to affect the outer_func’s y from inner_func, we would use (and not have a global y at all in this example). This demonstrates that global and nonlocal apply to different scopes. In summary, nonlocal is for the nearest enclosing function scope, and global is for the module scope.

(If we changed to use instead, it would have modified the outer_func’s y from 10 to 1, and left the global y at 100. But nonlocal wouldn’t be allowed here unless we remove the global y or use a different name, because currently the nearest enclosing scope’s y is 10 (outer_func’s y), which is fine, but we also have a global y which might confuse the scenario. Generally, avoid using the same name for globals and enclosing variables to prevent confusion.)

18. The Statement (Python 2) / Function (Python 3)

In Python 2, was a that printed to standard output. In Python 3, became a . While semantically similar (both output text), the syntax differs. The old print statement in Python 2 looked like: or (without parentheses and with special rules for ending comma or >> for redirection). The new print function is used like . The purpose in both cases is to display text or representations of objects to the console (or another file/stream if specified). Because Python 3's print is a function, it has flexible arguments: print(*objects, sep=' ', end='\n', file=sys.stdout, flush=False). The transition was made to make more consistent and powerful (and to remove it as a keyword).

The print statement/function is like a for your program. It's how your program communicates out to the world (the console, in most cases). In Python 2, it was like a built-in command with its own syntax. In Python 3, it’s like using a megaphone that you can configure (the print function, which is a tool you call with parentheses and can adjust its settings like separator, end character, etc.).

Originally, in Python 1 and 2, was one of the few statements (like , , etc.) that weren't just assignments or flow control but actually performed output. It was very convenient but had some quirks (for example, with a trailing comma in Python 2 would suppress the newline, and syntax was used to direct output to a file). With Python 3.0, as part of a larger effort to make the language more uniform, was changed to a normal function (PEP 3105) (PEP 3105 – Make print a function | peps.python.org). This meant removing it from the syntax as a special-case keyword. The change allowed features like specifying the end or separator string directly as function parameters instead of using special syntax. It was a significant backward-incompatible change. For a period, Python 2.6+ allowed from __future__ import print_function so that you could use the Python 3 style in Python 2 (What’s New In Python 3.0 — Python 3.13.2 documentation). As of Python 3, is entirely a function, and any usage of it as a statement (without parentheses) is a syntax error. The historical shift also means that in Python 3, you can override or rebind the name (because it's just a variable in builtins) if you wanted, whereas in Python 2 it was a keyword and couldn't be easily overridden. The function in Python 3 gained additional flexibility (like to forcibly flush output). Over time (Python 3.7+), remains essentially the same function. The key point is that code from Python 2 that uses statements needs to be converted to calls for Python 3. This is one of the most visible syntax changes between the versions.

Example 1: Python 2 style print vs Python 3 print()

The following shows how one would print in Python 2 versus Python 3. (This is for illustration; the Python 2 style will not run in Python 3 without the future import.)

In Python 2, you could write and it would print (with a space by default between arguments, and a newline at the end). In Python 3, the equivalent is . The output shown is for the Python 3 code. If you tried the Python 2 line in Python 3, you’d get a syntax error. In Python 2, the print statement automatically adds a space between items and a newline at the end, similar to the print function’s default behavior. The difference is mostly syntactical here.

Example 2: Using print() function features (Python 3)

Python 3’s print being a function offers more control. Here we demonstrate using the (separator) and parameters, which were not available with the old print statement (at least not in as clear a way).

  Monday | Tuesday | Wednesday  
  Hello World!

In the first print call, we printed three words separated by . Normally, print separates multiple arguments with a space by default, but by specifying , we changed it. The result is those words joined by . In the second and third print calls, we demonstrate changing the argument. The first prints "Hello" but ends with a space instead of a newline. Therefore, the next print’s output will continue on the same line. The next print("World", end="!\n") prints "World" and ends with an exclamation and a newline (we explicitly put ). The combined output of these two prints is "Hello World!" on one line (the first print output "Hello " then second output "World!"). In Python 2, to achieve the same effect, you might have written (with a comma to avoid newline) and then etc., which was less flexible. The Python 3 approach is more powerful and clear.

Example 3: Legacy Python 2 print to file vs Python 3

In Python 2, you could direct print output to a file using the syntax print >>file_obj, "message". In Python 3, you do this by print("message", file=file_obj). For example:

(to standard error, not standard output)

This will print the message to the standard error stream (which in many environments appears separately from standard output). In Python 2, you’d write print >> sys.stderr, "Error occurred!". The new syntax is more consistent with how you pass other parameters to functions. This example shows the modern way, leveraging the parameter of the print function.

19. The Statement (Python 2) / Function (Python 3)

was a that executed a string of Python code dynamically. In Python 3, is a function (actually a built-in) that does the same thing. The functionality is to take Python code, typically as a string (or compiled code object), and execute it as if it were part of the program. The syntax in Python 2 was or exec code_str in globals_dict, locals_dict for specifying context. In Python 3, you call exec(code_str, globals_dict, locals_dict) as a function (it’s actually a built-in that acts like a function). allows dynamic execution of Python code, which means you can construct code at runtime and run it. It has access to the current scopes via optional dictionaries for global and local namespace. It returns (it doesn’t produce a value, it just executes code for its side effects).

is like a embedded in your program. It’s as if your program can write a script on the fly and then run that script immediately. This is powerful but also potentially dangerous if used with untrusted input (like letting someone type code that you exec can run arbitrary code). So, it’s akin to having a magic book where any text you write becomes real actions in your program.

In Python 2, was a keyword/statement. You would use it without parentheses. For example: would execute that code, assigning 5 to x. In Python 3, as part of the language changes (PEP 3105 made print a function, and similarly PEP 3110 and others cleaned up exception syntax and exec), was removed as a statement and turned into a function-like built-in (What’s New In Python 3.0 — Python 3.13.2 documentation. Now you do . The functionality is largely the same, but as a function it takes arguments for the global and local dictionaries to execute in. The removal of as a statement also closed some subtle parsing issues and made the grammar simpler. Another note: Python 2 had an function for directly executing a file’s content; in Python 3, you achieve that via . Over the years, direct use of exec has been discouraged except for specific dynamic needs, because it can make code harder to understand and maintain, and it poses security risks if not handled carefully. But it remains a powerful tool. For example, some code generators or educational tools might use exec to run generated code. Historically, the statement in Python 2 couldn’t be easily sandboxed (and similarly for exec in Python 3, true sandboxing is hard in pure Python). The conversion to a function didn’t drastically change how exec works under the hood; it primarily changed syntax. One difference is that in Python 2, could be used in a local scope and it would by default modify that local scope. In Python 3, because exec is a function, modifying local variables of the caller via exec is tricky (exec on a string in the current local scope doesn’t automatically write to the local variables of the caller due to how Python’s scoping works; you typically provide a context dict). This is a subtle difference that one might encounter when porting code. But for the most part, in Python 3 has the same effect as in Python 2 in a global context or when using an explicit dictionary. Summarily, the big historical change was turning it from a statement to a function in Python 3, as part of making the language more regular.

Example 1: Executing dynamically created code

We have a string containing Python code. We use to run it. After exec, any definitions made by that code (variables, functions) will be available in our scope (if we exec in the global namespace).

Explanation: The string contains three lines of Python code. The call executes those lines as if they were written in the program at that point. It creates variables and , adds them, and prints the result. We see 15 printed. After this, we could also access and in our Python environment (they would exist in the global namespace following exec). This demonstrates how exec can define variables on the fly. Note that using exec like this can be risky if comes from an untrusted source, because it can execute arbitrary commands. But it's useful for dynamic programming tasks.

Example 2: Using exec to define a function dynamically

This example shows exec creating a new function at runtime and then calling it. We compose a function definition as a string, execute it, and then use the newly created function.

Here, the string in defines a function . After , the function exists in the current global namespace. We then call and it prints "Hello, Bob". This shows how exec can be used to generate functions or classes dynamically. While you could achieve similar results by other means (like using closures or factory functions), exec provides a very direct way to materialize code from text. Again, for security and clarity, this is typically done in controlled situations.

Example 3: Using exec with a custom namespace

In this example, we’ll execute code in a separate dictionary to avoid polluting our global namespace.

x in global? False  
x in namespace? 42

We created an empty dictionary . By passing to , the code executes such that is set in that dictionary rather than in our global variables. After exec, we check that is not in globals (so our module’s global scope was untouched), but in the namespace dict, is 42. This way, exec can be used in a sandboxed manner (to some extent) or at least to control where the executed code has effect. If the code had a print statement, it would still print to standard output, but variables and definitions went into that provided dictionary. Using exec(code, globals_dict, locals_dict) allows fine control of execution context.

This example underscores that in Python 3 is a function that can take separate global and local dictionaries. In Python 2, one would write exec code in globals_dict, locals_dict with slightly different syntax. The outcome is the same idea.

Got it! Here's the section in that style:

Next Steps with Python Simple Statements

You’ve just gone through a full guide on . Now’s the time to make it stick. Keep this close—print it out, bookmark it, or read through it during lunch. The goal is to make these statements feel like second nature.

Set aside a paper copy. There’s something about seeing it on the page. Mark it up. Highlight the parts that trip you up. Keep it at your desk or in your bag.

Take five or ten minutes when you can. A lunch break or a quiet moment is perfect. Skim the examples. See if you can spot how they work without looking at the notes.

Bookmark for Quick Checks

Save this guide in your browser. When you’re coding and hit a wall—check back. Forgot how works? Need a reminder on ? It’s all here.

Pick one statement a day. Write it out. Change it. Break it. Fix it. You don’t need a big project—just a few lines to get the feel for it.

Watch for Them in the Wild

As you read other people’s code, notice which simple statements show up. How do they use them? What tricks do they have? Borrow what works.

Stick with it. Small steps add up fast. Before long, these statements won’t just make sense—they’ll feel like old friends.

What’s Your Take on Python Simple Statements?

Now that you've seen how work, I'd love to hear what you think. Are there any statements you reach for often? Are some still a bit unclear?

What’s been the most useful or surprising in your own work? Have you found any clever ways to use them?

Share your thoughts below. Your tips and questions help others learn, too. What do you like?

is an American software engineer and writer based in Los Angeles. Mike writes about technology leadership and holds degrees in Linguistics and Industrial Automation. More about Mike Vincent

items.title: Python while Loops: Repeating Tasks Conditionally
items.author: Real Python
items.content:
Python’s loop enables you to execute a block of code repeatedly as long as a given condition remains true. Unlike loops, which iterate a known number of times, loops are ideal for situations where the number of iterations isn’t known upfront.

Loops are a pretty useful construct in Python, so learning how to write and use them is a great skill for you as a Python developer.

By the end of this tutorial, you’ll understand that:

  • used to initiate a loop that repeats a block of code as long as a condition is true.
  • A loop works by evaluating a condition at the start of each iteration. If the condition is true, then the loop executes. Otherwise, it terminates.
  • loops are useful when the number of iterations is unknown, such as waiting for a condition to change or continuously processing user input.
  • in Python creates an infinite loop that continues until a statement or external interruption occurs.
  • Python lacks a built-in do-while loop, but you can emulate it using a loop with a statement for conditional termination.

With this knowledge, you’re prepared to write effective loops in your Python programs, handling a wide range of iteration needs.

Test your knowledge with our interactive “Python while Loops: Repeating Tasks Conditionally” quiz. You’ll receive a score upon completion to help you track your learning progress:

In this quiz, you'll test your understanding of Python's while loop. This loop allows you to execute a block of code repeatedly as long as a given condition remains true. Understanding how to use while loops effectively is a crucial skill for any Python developer.

Getting Started With Python Loops

In programming, loops are statements that allow you to repeat a given set of operations a number of times. In practice, you’ll find two main types of loops:

  1. loops are mostly used to iterate a number of times, which is common when you’re processing data collections with a specific number of data items.
  2. loops are commonly used to iterate an number of times, which is useful when the number of iterations depends on a given condition.

Python has both of these loops and in this tutorial, you’ll learn about . In Python, you’ll generally use loops when you need to repeat a series of tasks an unknown number of times.

Python loops are compound statements with a and a that runs until a given condition becomes false. The basic syntax of a loop is shown below:

In this syntax, is an expression that the loop evaluates for its truth value. If the condition is true, then the loop body runs. Otherwise, the loop terminates. Note that the loop body can consist of one or more statements that must be indented properly.

Here’s a more detailed breakdown of this syntax:

  • is the keyword that initiates the loop header.
  • is an expression evaluated for truthiness that defines the exit condition.
  • consists of one or more statements to execute in each iteration.

Here’s a quick example of how you can use a loop to iterate over a decreasing sequence of numbers:

In this example, is the loop condition. If this condition returns a false value, the loop terminates. The body consists of a call to that displays the value on the screen. Next, you decrease the value of . This change will produce a different result when the loop evaluates the condition in the next iteration.

The loop runs while the condition remains true. When the condition turns false, the loop terminates, and the program execution proceeds to the first statement after the loop body. In this example, the loop terminates when reaches a value less than or equal to .

If the loop condition doesn’t become false, then you have a potentially infinite loop. Consider the following loop, and keep your fingers near the key combination to terminate its execution:

In this example, the loop condition is . This condition works when you decrease by . However, if you decrease it by 2, the condition may never become false, resulting in a potentially infinite loop. In such cases, you can usually terminate the loop by pressing , which raises a exception on most operating systems.

Note that the loop checks its condition first before anything else happens. If it’s false to start with, then the loop body will never run:

items.title: I left corporate and still do roadmaps + a Memphis update
items.author: Jones Beach
items.content:
This month I am putting a bow on my Q1 roadmap and continuing to unify the two Memphis execution engines.

I chose an update post because I want to “build in public.” What follows is what has been on my mind! I don’t write advice posts (Why you shouldn’t use ) or random technical overviews (Vectors vs. Slices in Rust: A Complete Guide) because I am unable to do either with a straight face. If I could, I’d probably still work in a large organization where those kind of ambitious but safe norms are politely celebrated. Someone else will write those pieces and I support them in the same way I support someone running a marathon; I’m not against it, but that doesn’t mean I want to.

It appears this “build in public” includes me breaking the fourth wall on my writing process! Can you tell I struggle in groups? If you’re still here, let’s continue.

I began Q1 with a list of 6 items I wanted to achieve for From Scratch. As someone who once flew to Chicago to write a list of personal goals on a public library whiteboard with a close friend, I’m no stranger to making goals. What felt new was doing them with that uniquely American fuel: a profit motive.

The Original 6 (the little-known prequel to The Magnificent 7) were:

  • Launch website testimonials (thanks Jakub!)
  • Run a small trial with paid ads
  • Write chapters 3-5 of my novella
  • File taxes and pay Q1 estimated taxes

I would later add two more:

  • Add CTA to and modernize my personal site
  • Create a runbook for follow-ups

I’m still working on Chapter 5 (Chapters 1-4 are on From Scratch Press) and the follow-up runbook, but everything else is . Who knew you could accomplish things without Jira for Enterprise.

These records have unintentionally produced a fairly detailed account of the playbook I’m running to build my online business. Which I’m excited to share with you in case you too are interested in how to earn small amounts of money with only an internet connection.

I feel pride looking at this list because it makes my efforts feel less random. I can reassure myself Sure, I’m still growing, but here’s my strategy. I can (and have!) thrown this list into ChatGPT and asked what foundational pieces am I missing. And each time it says “Share your work on social media,” I close the tab and search for From Scratch on Google instead.

Multiple Execution Engines

Memphis has supported two execution engines for about a year now. But barely.

The treewalk interpreter is farthest along and, if you wanted to try to run real code, what you should use. I’ve been strengthening the bytecode VM’s foundation and it’s coming along but slowly. Because I have no deadlines, I’m being deliberate to define Memphis capabilities versus treewalk capabilities versus bytecode VM capabilities. Did I mention I have no deadlines?

The unification has proceeded in the following broad strokes.

Initially, you could pick an engine like this.


memphis example.py

vm memphis example.py

Which works fine! But after kicking off the runtime, there was no coming back together. Or reunification.

This remains the interface to select a non-default engine, but I’m gradually unifying more of the code behind the scenes. I’ve also floated the idea of using a Rust feature flag instead of an environment variable to produce a smaller binary.

Because I respect those who came before me, I wanted to treat Python runtime errors as first-class. Meaning I wouldn’t trap them below deck after hitting an iceberg.

Instead of separate error types for treewalk and VM errors, I would combine them. This would also be a chance to turn this dumping ground I aspirationally called into a type which actually represented possible Python runtime errors.

I landed on this streamlined structure which could now be used on both engines. All my previous , , etc., variants are now just a . This mirrors CPython, where an optional parameter provides more detail.

With this unified type, I was able to test for an expected in a crosscheck test. Which reminds me, I should really write more about crosscheck, my testing framework for both engines. I have a fun proc macro in the works there.

Now that both engines returned an , I needed to build an interface to push new stack frames to the . These would be displayed to the user whenever a Python runtime error occurs in their user code.

I’m still actively working on unifying stack traces, but I’m excited because it is me saying “Memphis supports a debug stack trace, regardless of what execution engine you choose,” rather than just “both engines have a stack trace.” Do you see the difference? It’s a pLaTfOrM. Or maybe I mean fRaMeWoRk? It’s something.

I cleaned up my and structs and moved them into a module to indicate they represent a Python stack trace, but should be used as a source of runtime info for an engine.

One challenge is giving each engine access to the right-sized shared state object. This would be how each engine could register new stack frames; think something like state.push_stack_frame(function.to_stack_frame()) when entering a new function context. I’m attempting to balance platform capabilities () against freedom of implementation within each engine ( and ).

The other challenge of implementing stack traces is it forces you to keep around your metadata for use at the right time. Metadata like file paths and line numbers aren’t necessary for actually running Python code, but they’re essential for debugging. When a statement is parsed and immediately evaluated (treewalk) or immediately compiled (bytecode VM, though this has a bug right now), we record the line number of the start of the current function and increment it each time we see a new line. This parser<>runtime communication is only necessary for stack traces (for me. so far.).

This work stream has been a crash course in applying the Single-Responsibility Principle a few decades after I first learned of it. It’s easy to understand the definition (”of course each function/struct/class only does one thing!”). But applying it? That’s harder (”we’ve got access to state here so I’ll put it there!”). The result is my code is GRADUALLY shifting from a medium number of medium-sized functions to a whole lot of tiny ones. I’ve always been an advocate for adding a second entrypoint, either through unit tests or a REPL, but Memphis has forced me to expand that thinking to an entirely different level.

I’m putting the finishing touches on my Q2 roadmap, which should take my business to the max! Do people still say that?

I recently finished by Paul Millerd and his message of finding the work each of us wants to do indefinitely resonated with me. With my Memphis engine work and my roadmap work, I believe I’m closer than ever to finding that. I’d also love to be a technical mentor to anyone reading this. Because money.

Subscribe & Save [on nothing]

Want a software career that actually feels ? I wrote a free 5-day email course on honing your craft, aligning your work with your values, and building for yourself. Or just not hating your job! Get it here.

I mentor software engineers to navigate technical challenges and career growth in a supportive, sometimes silly environment. If you’re interested, you can explore my mentorship programs.

In addition to mentoring, I also write about neurodivergence and self-employment. Less code and the same number of jokes.

items.title: Real-World Applications of Machine Learning
items.author: Devraj More
items.content:
Machine learning (ML) is transforming industries worldwide by enabling computers to learn from data and make intelligent decisions. From healthcare to finance, businesses are leveraging ML to enhance efficiency, improve accuracy, and drive innovation. If you're interested in mastering machine learning and applying it to real-world scenarios, enrolling in a data science certification course in Delhi can be a game-changer.

What is Machine Learning?

Machine learning is a subset of artificial intelligence (AI) that allows computers to recognize patterns, predict outcomes, and make data-driven decisions without explicit programming. It uses algorithms trained on historical data to improve performance over time.

Top Real-World Applications of Machine Learning

  1. Healthcare & Medical Diagnosis

Machine learning is revolutionizing the healthcare industry by improving diagnosis, treatment, and patient care.

Disease Prediction & Diagnosis: ML models analyze medical images, lab reports, and genetic data to detect diseases like cancer, diabetes, and heart conditions.

Personalized Treatment Plans: AI-driven models recommend customized treatment plans based on patient history and real-time health data.

Drug Discovery: Pharmaceutical companies use ML to accelerate drug development, reducing research time and costs.

Example: IBM Watson Health uses machine learning to assist doctors in diagnosing diseases more accurately and efficiently.

Machine learning enhances financial security, investment strategies, and fraud detection.

Fraud Detection: ML algorithms identify fraudulent transactions in real time by detecting unusual patterns.

Credit Scoring & Risk Assessment: Banks assess loan eligibility and creditworthiness using ML models.

Algorithmic Trading: Hedge funds and investment firms leverage ML to predict stock market trends and automate trading decisions.

Example: JPMorgan Chase uses ML-powered fraud detection systems to monitor transactions and prevent financial crimes.

ML plays a crucial role in enhancing customer experience and optimizing business operations.

Personalized Recommendations: Platforms like Amazon and Netflix use ML to suggest products and content based on user preferences.

Dynamic Pricing: Retailers adjust prices dynamically based on demand, competition, and customer behavior.

Chatbots & Virtual Assistants: AI-powered chatbots provide customer support and handle inquiries efficiently.

Example: Amazon's recommendation engine, powered by machine learning, drives over 35% of its total sales.

  1. Manufacturing & Supply Chain Optimization

ML helps manufacturers streamline operations, improve quality control, and predict demand.

Predictive Maintenance: Sensors in machinery collect data to predict and prevent equipment failures.

Quality Control: AI-powered visual inspection systems detect product defects with high accuracy.

Demand Forecasting: Retailers optimize inventory management by predicting future demand using ML models.

Example: General Electric uses ML for predictive maintenance, reducing downtime and operational costs.

  1. Autonomous Vehicles & Transportation

Self-driving cars and intelligent transportation systems rely heavily on machine learning.

Autonomous Vehicles: Companies like Tesla and Waymo use ML for object detection, route planning, and decision-making.

Traffic Management: AI analyzes traffic patterns to optimize signal timing and reduce congestion.

Ride-Sharing Optimization: Apps like Uber and Lyft use ML to match drivers with riders and predict ETA.

Example: Tesla's Autopilot feature leverages deep learning and computer vision to enhance autonomous driving capabilities.

  1. Social Media & Content Recommendation

Social media platforms use ML to personalize user experiences and detect harmful content.

Content Curation: Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok recommend posts and videos based on user behavior.

Spam & Fake News Detection: AI identifies and removes harmful or misleading content.

Sentiment Analysis: Brands analyze user sentiment on social media to improve marketing strategies.

Example: YouTube’s ML algorithm suggests videos based on user preferences, maximizing engagement.

  1. Cybersecurity & Threat Detection

ML enhances cybersecurity by identifying threats and preventing data breaches.

Intrusion Detection Systems: AI monitors network traffic for suspicious activities.

Phishing & Malware Detection: ML-based tools detect and block malicious websites and emails.

Behavioral Analysis: AI identifies unusual user behavior to prevent insider threats.

Example: Companies like Symantec and Palo Alto Networks use ML-driven cybersecurity solutions to protect against cyber threats.

Machine learning is reshaping education by providing personalized learning experiences.

Adaptive Learning Platforms: AI customizes study plans based on students' learning patterns.

Automated Grading: ML automates essay and exam grading, saving educators time.

Virtual Tutors: AI-driven chatbots provide instant support to students.

Example: Coursera and Duolingo use ML to enhance personalized learning experiences.

  1. Agriculture & Precision Farming

Machine learning is transforming agriculture by optimizing farming techniques and improving yield prediction.

Crop Monitoring: AI-powered drones analyze soil and crop health.

Pest & Disease Detection: ML identifies crop diseases early, preventing losses.

Weather Prediction: Farmers use AI-driven weather forecasting to plan cultivation effectively.

Example: John Deere uses AI-powered sensors for precision farming, optimizing crop yields.

Why Learn Machine Learning?

Building ML models using Python, TensorFlow, and Scikit-learn.

Working on real-world projects to develop problem-solving skills.

Mastering data preprocessing, feature engineering, and model evaluation.

Preparing for lucrative job roles like Data Scientist, ML Engineer, and AI Specialist.

Conclusion: Take the First Step Towards a Machine Learning Career

Machine learning is transforming industries, making it one of the most sought-after skills in today’s digital world. Whether you’re looking to work in healthcare, finance, e-commerce, or cybersecurity, mastering ML can open doors to exciting opportunities.

items.title: Building a Social Platform with FastAPI & Neo4j: Lessons from a Side Project
items.author: Daniel Mayo
items.content:
Firstly let me introduce the side project I'm working on. I'll keep it brief, because I want to get to the tech part of the discussion.

So, I see the direction that social media has been going lately and I don't like what I see. I won't go into details, but I know I'm not the only one.

Anyway, I thought it would be an interesting experiment / hobby project to try to create one myself from scratch.

So let's dive into how I'm building it, the decisions and challenges along the way.

There's a lot of split opinions on how good AI is at coding. Or rather, I should say LLMs.

But right from the get go ChatGPT has been my trusty development partner. It came up with the initial name and concept, and helped me get started with the most basic version. Honestly I would have been too lazy / busy to start this project otherwise.

I will henceforth refer to ChatGPT and I as "we" 😁

Copilot is my second side kick. I'm a bit of a contrarian and I use Zed as my editor these days, which comes with copilot integration. I just don't like VSCode 🤷‍♂️

First decision that I made was to use Neo4j for the database.

Why not just use a relational database I hear you cry?

Well, first of all a disclaimer: I work for Neo4j.

Secondly, a graph database is tailor made for storing and querying social connections. Not that I have any users to connect at the moment 😂

Thirdly, why not? For me it's more fun and easier to use. I do have an appreciation for Postgres, but I just find Neo4j more fun and freeing to work with.

Oh and there's a free managed version you can spin up very easily (that I helped build). This was the biggest thing for me on this project: cheap and easy.

Anyway, I'm not really here to promote Neo4j. That's just what I'm using. On to the next choice.

Since I'm working a lot with Python these days and I find it quite pleasant and easy to use, it was quite natural for ChatGPT to suggest a Python backend, specifically FastAPI.

I've not used it before, only Flask (which I'm not the biggest fan of).

ChatGPT created the initial version along with Poetry for dependency management and I gave it a spin.

I was instantly impressed. This is a very nice framework to use for getting started with. You can get something up and running super fast, while also supporting async requests, background tasks, and websockets for scalability.

Why not Node.js or something like that? Well, I've used it before and honestly I wasn't a fan. I prefer JS/TS on the front-end. Just my preference.

I could have also used Go or Java or something else, but that sounds much less fun for getting started quickly on a new project.

So, at this point I have a working API that stores data in Neo4j. A headless social network if you will.

Now I could create an Android/iOS client, but that's not my thing. So web app it is.

I ask ChatGPT to knock up a quick front-end. We decide to avoid any heavy dependencies at the moment, so vanilla JS it is. No React etc.

We decide to use Tailwind for CSS styling. No Bootstrap, no component lib. A very natural and solid choice for quick iteration.

No Vite build or whatever. Just pure untransformed static files. The lack of build time or ceremony is very refreshing!

The first version looks terrible of course. But it works!

Okay, so it's time to add login capabilities.

I don't want to roll my own, I don't want anything heavy. I want easy, and free.

I decide to integrate Auth0 with Google OIDC as the one initial login method for now.

Not as easy as I'd hoped, but eventually after a lot of back and forth I got it to work.

6. Deployment Pt. 1: Render

We arrived on using Render.io to deploy to the world. It was quick, easy, and best of all free to get started.

It was pretty great for testing at first.

But there's a catch: it suspends when not in use and the cold start times can be 50 seconds plus!

I mean I don't blame them. You can upgrade to solve that problem.

But it bugged me enough that I wanted to look at other options. Which led me to...

7. Deployment Pt. 2: D.O.

I switched to Digital Oceans App Platform. For a relatively low monthly fee, it solved what I was looking for.

Compared to Render it was an absolute pain to set up.

Eventually I got it working though and now it's set up it's easy to use. I just git push to deploy.

Okay, so I wanted a custom domain now. I wanted to use a .social TLD.

Of course my working title up until this point was already taken: ripple.social

However, rpl.social was free. ChatGPT encouraged me to search for existing Trademarks. This was a pain, but eventually I found a clash. No go.

Next up: ChatGPT suggested relay.social

Not bad I guess. But taken. Next it suggested RelayHub.social and RelayChat.social

Neither were amazing but they were free. I went with the latter. Only after registering did I realise it sounds like Internet Relay Chat (IRC!)

Oh well, it's not great but it'll do for now.

Naming things is really hard!!

Now, I have no users. No big surprise 😂

I'm trying to get some, but without some kind of analytics I wouldn't know whether anyone is visiting or dropping off.

I wanted something cheap while being GDPR compliant and privacy respecting. Swetrix ticked this box and is pleasantly simple to use. It tracks stats anonymously, without cookies or PII.

As a bonus it comes with anonymous error reporting as well. Nice.

Now that's where I am now. The app is very feature light, and very much a work in progress. Rather than focussing any more on making the design look less terrible, add features or polish, I thought I'd try to look for some users and get some feedback.

As Reid Hoffman said: "If you are not embarrassed by the first version of your product, you've launched too late."

Thanks for reading, well if you make it this far I'm impressed.

I'd love to hear about your own side projects.

What would you have done differently to me if you were in my shoes? Any tips or suggestions?

Any thoughts or questions about what I did?

items.title: Efficient Prime Number Calculation in Python
items.author: Dmitry Romanoff
items.content:
Prime numbers are fundamental in mathematics and have significant applications in fields like cryptography and computer science. This article explores an efficient approach to calculating the sum of prime numbers up to a given number using Python.

Understanding Prime Numbers

A prime number is a natural number greater than 1 that has no positive divisors other than 1 and itself. Examples include 2, 3, 5, 7, etc. Identifying primes efficiently can be challenging, especially for large numbers.

Let's break down the provided code:

    • This function checks if a number is prime.
    • Numbers less than 2 are not prime.
    • It iterates from 2 up to the square root of to check for factors.
    • This function calculates the sum of all primes up to .
    • It uses a loop to iterate through each number from 2 to , checking if it's prime using .

The code includes an example where the user is prompted for input, and the result is printed. For instance:

Optimizations in Prime Calculation

  1. Square Root Optimization:

    • Checking for factors only up to the square root of reduces the number of iterations significantly.

  2. Efficient Factor Checking:

    • Instead of checking all numbers, we can first check if 2 is a factor and then iterate through odd numbers starting from 3.

Implementation with Sieve of Eratosthenes

The Sieve of Eratosthenes is an ancient algorithm for finding all primes up to any given limit. It's more efficient, especially for larger values of . Here's how it works:

  1. Create a list of boolean values initialized to , representing whether each number is prime.
  2. Set entries at indices 0 and 1 to since they are not primes.
  3. For each number starting from 2, if it's still marked as prime, mark all its multiples as non-prime.
  4. The remaining values represent primes.

While the initial approach with works correctly, it's less efficient for large numbers compared to methods like the Sieve of Eratosthenes. For practical applications requiring performance, especially with larger values of , implementing sieve-based algorithms is advisable.

Choosing between these methods depends on the specific requirements: if simplicity is preferred, stick with . For better performance in handling large inputs, use the sieve method. Always consider edge cases and ensure your code efficiently handles them based on its intended use.

items.title: Scalable APIs with Django and Microservices [Part 1]
items.author: Tech Tales
items.content:
As applications grow, monolithic architectures often struggle with scalability, maintainability, and performance. Microservices architecture presents a robust solution by dividing applications into independent, self-contained services that can be developed, deployed, and scaled separately.

This guide will walk you through transitioning from a monolithic Django API to a scalable

microservices-based API architecture. By the end of this series, you’ll be equipped with the necessary tools to design, build, and deploy microservices effectively using Django REST Framework (DRF).

Understanding Microservices Architecture

Microservices architecture is an approach where an API system is built as a collection of loosely coupled services, each handling a specific business functionality. These services communicate via lightweight protocols like REST APIs or message queues.

Beneflts of Microservices:

  • Independent Development & Deployment: Each API service can be updated without impacting others.

  • Different services can use different programming languages and frameworks.

  • A failure in one service does not impact the entire API system.

Why Use Django for Microservices?

Django, traditionally used for monolithic applications, can effectively support microservices due to its modularity, ORM, and strong ecosystem.

  • Django’s built-in features expedite API development.

  • Django apps can be structured as independent microservices.

  • Compatible with Celery, Redis, Docker, and Kubernetes.

  • Easily integrates with Django REST Framework (DRF), GraphQL, and message brokers like Kafka and RabbitMQ.

Transitioning from Monolith to Microservices

Monolithic API Architecture:

  • Simpler to develop and maintain initially.

  • Easier debugging with centralized logs.

  • Suitable for small API applications.

  • Dimcult to scale individual components.

  • Deployment changes impact the entire system.

  • Limited flexibility in adopting new technologies.

When to Move to Microservices:

  • The API is experiencing performance bottlenecks.

  • Multiple development teams need autonomy over different API features.

  • The need for technology diversity arises across different services.

  • Setting Up the Base API Architecture

  • Building Your First Microservice with Django REST Framework

  • Implementing Service Communication

  • Managing Databases in Microservices

  • Deploying API Services with Docker and Kubernetes

  • Securing API Microservices

  • Monitoring and Scaling API Services

Step 1: Establishing the Base API Architecture

To transition into microservices, we’ll first create a foundational Django monolithic API and identify separable components.

Setting Up Microservices API

We will break our API system into three microservices:

  • Authentication Service - Handles user registration and authentication.
  • Inventory Service -Manages products and stock.
  • Order Service - Manages orders and payment transactions.

Creating Django Projects for Each Microservice 

mkdir microservices cd microservices
django-admin startproject auth_service

cd auth_service && django-admin startapp accounts cd ..

django-admin startproject inventory_service

cd inventory_service && django-admin startapp inventory cd ..
django-admin startproject order_service

cd order_service && django-admin startapp orders

Conflguring Each Microservice

Authentication Service (auth_service/accounts/models.py):

from django.contrib.auth.models import AbstractUser class CustomUser(AbstractUser):
phone_number = models.CharField(max_length=15, unique=True)

Inventory Service (inventory_service/inventory/models.py):

from django.db import models class Item(models.Model):
name = models.CharField(max_length=200)

price = models.DecimalField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2) stock = models.IntegerField()

Order Service (order_service/orders/models.py):

from django.db import models
from auth_service.accounts.models import CustomUser from inventory_service.inventory.models import Item
class Order(models.Model):
user = models.ForeignKey(CustomUser, on_delete=models.CASCADE) item = models.ForeignKey(Item, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
quantity = models.IntegerField()
created_at = models.DateTimeField(auto_now_add=True)

Implementing REST APIs for Microservices

Each service will expose APIs using Django REST Framework (DRF).

Authentication Service API (auth_service/accounts/views.py):

from rest_framework import viewsets from .models import CustomUser
from .serializers import UserSerializer
class UserViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet): queryset = CustomUser.objects.all() serializer_class = UserSerializer

Inventory Service API (inventory_service/inventory/views.py):

from rest_framework import viewsets from .models import Item
from .serializers import ItemSerializer
class ItemViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet): queryset = Item.objects.all()
serializer_class = ItemSerializer

Order Service API (order_service/orders/views.py):

from rest_framework import viewsets from .models import Order
from .serializers import OrderSerializer
class OrderViewSet(viewsets.ModelViewSet): queryset = Order.objects.all()
serializer_class = OrderSerializer

Running Migrations for Each Service

cd auth_service && python manage.py makemigrations && python manage.py migrate
cd ../inventory_service && python manage.py makemigrations && python manage.py migrate cd ../order_service && python manage.py makemigrations && python manage.py migrate

Deploying API Microservices with Docker

Every microservice will include a dedicated Dockerfile. Example Dockerfile for Authentication Service:

FROM python:3.10 WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt COPY . .
CMD ["gunicorn", "auth_service.wsgi:application", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000"]

In this first installment, we introduced API microservices, set up three separate Django microservices, and implemented REST APIs for them. We also covered running migrations and containerizing each service with Docker. In Part 2, we will explore inter-service communication and authentication management.

items.title: How to Learn Python Programming Step by Step?
items.author: ankit_Cyber
items.content:
As there are so many programming languages, many people still want to learn Python because it’s easy to learn and as simple as learning the English language. Its vast applications, from machine learning, gaming, and cybersecurity to AI, make it one of the most in-demand skills today.

If you are asking how to learn Python programming step by step, then in these articles, we have discussed some proven strategies and processes to learn Python from a beginner level to advanced. So, without wasting any time, let’s move on to the main topic.

What is Python Programming?

Python programming is a high-level programming language that is known for its simplicity and versatility. It is one of the most popular programming languages in the world, as it is used in a wide range from web development, data analysis, artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), automation, and more, making it one of the best languages.

Why Python is so Popular?

  1. — Python is very simple and easy to learn, its syntax is like the English language. So, a beginner won’t find any difficulty to master it.
  2. Versatile and Multi-Purpose — Python application is huge, it is used in web development, artificial intelligence, data science and much more.
  3. — There’s a huge global python community that keeps things growing, offers support to each other, and gives you access to all kinds of resources.
  4. Extensive Libraries and Frameworks — Python provides robust libraries such as TensorFlow, Pandas, and Django, each designed for different applications.
  5. Cross-Platform and Scalable — It works smoothly on various operating systems and handles big projects really well.

Guide to Learn Python Programming Step by Step

Step 1: Understand the Basics of Python

Start with the fundamentals to create a strong foundation before going into more complex concepts:

  • : Download and install Python from python.org.
  • : Understand Python’s indentation rules, how to use comments, and the steps to run scripts.
  • Work with Variables and Data Types: Discover the world of integers, floats, strings, lists, tuples, dictionaries, and sets.
  • : Explore arithmetic, comparison, logical, and assignment operators.

If you are searching how to learn python programming step by step then learning these basics will help you feel more confident in writing simple Python programs and understanding its syntax.

Step 2: Control Flow and Loops

Control structures let you make decisions and run code over and over again:

  • : Use if, elif, and else to control the flow of execution based on conditions.
  • : Learn how to use for and while loops to iterate over sequences and perform repetitive tasks.
  • : Utilize break, continue, and pass for better control over loops.

Engaging in small projects like a number guessing game or a simple calculator will deepen your understanding of these concepts.

Step 3: Functions and Modular Programming

In this step of how to learn python programming step by step, we will discuss how functions can help organize code into reusable segments, which improve both structure and efficiency

  • Define and Call Functions: Use def to create functions and call them in your programs.
  • Function Arguments and Return Values: Learn how to pass parameters and return values from functions.
  • : Python provides various built-in functions like len(), max(), and sorted().
  • Create and Import Modules: Write modular code and reuse functions across different scripts.

If you get the hang of functions, you’ll be able to write cleaner and more efficient programs.

Step 5: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) and Libraries

OOP makes it easier to write scalable and maintainable code:

  • Create Classes and Objects: Learn how to define classes and create object instances.
  • Understand Encapsulation, Inheritance, and Polymorphism: These concepts improve code reusability and organization.

Python’s extensive libraries further enhance its capabilities:

  • : For numerical computations and data analysis.
  • : For data visualization.
  • Requests and BeautifulSoup: For web scraping.
  • : For web development.

Exploring these libraries expands your skillset and allows you to work on diverse projects.

Step 6: Build Projects and Explore Advanced Topics

After mastering the fundamentals, it’s crucial to implement your knowledge through practical projects

  • : Implement CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations.
  • : Extract data from websites using requests and BeautifulSoup.
  • : Use basic NLP (Natural Language Processing) techniques.

For further learning, explore:

  • : Use libraries like TensorFlow and Scikit-learn.
  • : Automate repetitive tasks using os and shutil.
  • : Build REST APIs using Flask or FastAPI.

Step 7: Consider Choosing a Python Course

A structured Python course accelerates learning through guided lessons, practical projects, and mentorship. Here are some recommended institutions offering Python courses:

  • : Best known for cybersecurity and ethical hacking courses along with Python training.
  • : Offers hands-on Python training, especially useful for beginners and professionals looking to upskill.
  • : Provides Python courses from reputed universities and instructors, covering beginner to advanced levels.

If you are still not getting answer of how to learn python programming step by step then choosing a good course can provide you with structured learning, practical experience, and industry-recognized certification.

Begin your journey in Python by systematically learning the fundamentals, practicing control structures and data structures, and developing projects. Delve into its extensive ecosystem to consistently improve your skills

After understanding on how to learn python programming step by step take next step by considering enrolling in a professional course. Structured training can enhance your learning experience, regardless of whether you are a beginner or looking to improve your skills.

🚀 Join a Python Programming Course Today! Get hands-on experience, mentorship, and certification to boost your career in software development, data science, or automation.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the best way to start learning Python? Start with the basics like syntax, loops, and functions, then practice through small projects and enroll in a structured course for guided learning.

How long does it take to learn Python? It depends on your dedication, but beginners can grasp the basics in 1–2 months, while mastering advanced topics may take 6 months or more with consistent practice.

Is Python good for a career in IT? Yes! Python is widely used in web development, data science, AI, cybersecurity, and automation, making it a valuable skill for IT professionals.

Why Python is so popular? Because its use in every industry from data analytics, AI, web development, automation, and machine learning, thus create a high demand.

What is the duration of the Python programming training course? The duration for python programming training in Delhi is 60 Hours.

items.title: Hiring: Job Title: Tier 1 AI/Data Engineer - LegalTech Startup (Rapid 2-Month Impact)
items.author: Geoffrey Lee
items.content:
Job Title: Tier 1 AI/Data Engineer - LegalTech Startup (Rapid 2-Month Impact)

About Maidalv: Maidalv is an AI-powered legal tech startup revolutionizing IP protection for e-commerce and cross-border enterprises. We're building a cutting-edge platform to help businesses proactively avoid costly cross-border IP infringement issues, protecting their brands and bottom line. We are pre-seed, highly ambitious, and moving incredibly fast.

About the Role: We are seeking an exceptionally talented and driven Tier 1 AI/Data Engineer to join us for a critical 2-month contract. It may lead to a long-term position in the company if parties enjoy working together. This is not your typical coding role. We need a strategic thinker who can quickly grasp our business, anticipate challenges, and deliver impactful solutions at lightning speed.

You will be instrumental in completing the core functionality of our platform. This involves building and optimizing our system for:

• Intelligent Data Collection: Developing and refining web scraping processes to gather comprehensive IP data (trademarks, copyrights, patents).

• AI-Powered Infringement Detection: Implementing and optimizing AI/RAG models (using TensorFlow/PyTorch, Langfuse) to analyze product listings against our IP database and identify potential infringements. This includes prompt engineering, model comparison, and performance tuning.

• Scalable Data Management: Ensuring efficient data storage, retrieval, and management using MySQL, with an eye towards future scalability.

• Platform Completion: Bringing all components together into a functional platform capable of generating actionable infringement risk assessments.

What We Mean by "Tier 1": For us, "Tier 1" goes far beyond just excellent programming skills. We are looking for someone who embodies the following:

• Business Acumen: Rapidly understands the startup's value proposition and how their technical contributions directly impact business success. Sees the bigger picture.

• Strategic & Proactive Thinking: Anticipates scalability issues, suggests optimal architectural patterns, identifies potential risks before they become problems, and proactively seeks opportunities to enhance the platform. Can envision future data value beyond immediate needs.

• Exceptional Problem Solver: Devises creative and effective solutions to novel challenges, pivots quickly when faced with roadblocks, and doesn't get stuck on a single approach. Excels at debugging and optimization.

• Ultra-Fast Execution: Operates at an accelerated pace, leveraging AI tools and efficient methodologies to achieve in days what might take others weeks. Quickly learns and adapts to new information and feedback. Focuses on delivering results, not just writing code.

• Startup DNA: Highly self-driven, thrives in a fast-paced, dynamic environment, takes ownership, and is comfortable with ambiguity. Possesses a 24/7 mentality and an aggressive drive to achieve ambitious goals within tight deadlines.

Key Responsibilities: • Rapidly onboard and understand the Maidalv business model and technical architecture.

• Take ownership of the end-to-end development and optimization of our IP data collection and infringement detection platform.

• Design, implement, and optimize web scraping strategies to gather comprehensive IP data.

• Develop and fine-tune AI/RAG models (TensorFlow/PyTorch, Langfuse) for accurate and efficient infringement identification.

• Optimize prompts and compare different models to achieve the highest performance.

• Ensure robust data management and scalability using MySQL.

• Proactively identify and solve technical challenges, including prompt optimization, model comparison, and data collection bottlenecks.

• Collaborate closely with the founding team, providing technical insights and contributing to strategic decisions.

• Deliver a fully functional and performant platform within the 2-month timeframe.

What We're Looking For: • Extensive experience (5+ years) as a Software Engineer or Data Scientist, with a strong focus on Python, AI/ML, and data management.

• Proven expertise in web scraping techniques and technologies.

• Deep understanding and practical experience with AI/RAG models (TensorFlow, PyTorch, Langfuse or similar frameworks).

• Solid experience with SQL databases, particularly MySQL, and data modeling.

• Experience with Flask or similar Python web frameworks.

• Demonstrable ability to quickly learn and adapt to new technologies and domains.

• Exceptional problem-solving and analytical skills.

• Strong communication and collaboration skills.

• A proactive, self-driven, and results-oriented work ethic.

• A "startup mindset" – comfortable with ambiguity, fast-paced environments, and taking ownership.

Bonus Points: • Experience with or strong interest in learning about IP law, particularly in the context of e-commerce.

• Familiarity with Temporary Restraining Orders (TROs) or other IP enforcement mechanisms.

• Experience optimizing GPU usage for AI/ML workloads, especially on budget-friendly cloud providers (like Hetzner).

• Contributions to open-source projects.

• Prior experience in early-stage startups.

Qualifications & Ideal Candidate Profile: • Education:

o Strongly Preferred: Master's degree in Computer Science from a top-tier university (Ivy League or equivalent), or demonstrable equivalent expertise through exceptional professional experience and achievements. (Instead of "equivalent degree", focus on "equivalent expertise")

• Professional Experience: o Strongly Preferred: Proven track record of success at a highly selective and demanding technology organization, such as:

Leading technology companies (e.g., FAANG, Palantir, unicorn startups known for engineering excellence) Top-tier hedge funds or financial institutions with significant technology focus.

Renowned research labs or institutions pushing the boundaries of AI and related fields.

o Relevant Experience is Key: Experience should demonstrate a strong focus on software engineering, AI/ML, data management, and ideally, experience with rapid development cycles and delivering impactful solutions in dynamic environments. (Emphasize relevant experience over just the name of the institution).

Technical Skills: (Keep the skills list from the previous job post) "Tier 1" Attributes: (Keep the "Tier 1" attributes section from the previous job post) This is a premium contract role with premium compensation, reflecting the need for Tier 1 talent and rapid impact.

Application Process: Resume submission and contact email: yumi.cho @maidalv.com and jslawpclegal@gmail.com. pls write your name and position seeking in the email subject. Thank you.

items.title: Flask Video Streaming App Tutorial
items.author: Ethan
items.content:
Hello! 😎 In this tutorial I will show you how to build a video streaming application using Flask and FFmpeg.

The application is designed as a Flask web server that manages video uploads, processes them using FFmpeg, and server adaptive streaming content to the end user. Adaptive streaming formats such as HLS (HTTP Live Streaming) and DASH (Dynamic Adaptive Streaming over HTTP) are essential for providing a smooth playback experience across varying network conditions.

  • File Uploading: Securely handling video file uploads, verifying file types and enforcing size limits.
  • Video Conversion: Converting uploaded videos to HLS and DASH formats using FFmpeg.
  • Streaming Endpoints: Providing endpoints that serve video streams to clients.
  • Modern UI: A responsive front-end that enables drag-and-drop file uploads, video listing and a dynamic video player.

Now that we have an overview of the application, let's start building the backend.🤓

Building The Back End With Flask

Flask is chosen for its lightweight nature and simplicity. The code leverages several libraries including os, uuid, subprocess, logging and extensions such as Flask-Cors for handling cross origin.

First, we need to create a Python virtual environment. This can be done and activated with the following command:

python3  venv /bin/activate

Next create a new file called main.py and populate it with the required imports:

Next we need to configure some variables for the directories and logging etc. Next add the following Python code:

Here we initialize the app and logging etc.

Next we need a helper function to ensure the uploaded file does not exceed 100MB and is a valid video format:

After that we will write a function that handles the conversion to HLS:

Now that we have a function to handle HLS we next need to handle the DASH side, below the above function add the following function:

Feel free to change any of the variables. Now that we've handled both HLS and DASH we can now start programming the API routes.

The first route is an index route that returns the index page:

After that we also need to handle a route for the user to upload a video file:

The above route c # For simplicity, we'll process synchronously in this example # In production, use a task queue like Celeryhecks that the file is valid and if so converts it to both HLS and DASH, if ok it returns both the HLS and DASH stream information.

Next we will create an endpoint for the video stream:

After that we will next create an endpoint that allows for the video to be played:

Lastly we will create an endpoint that allows the users to view all video files available:

At the end we need a main function to run the application:

Phew! Now that the backend is finally done we can now work on the frontend. 😆

First create a new folder called "templates" and inside that directory create a new file called "index.html" and populate it with the following:

Video Streaming AppVideo Streaming AppUpload VideoDrag  drop your video file or
                            Browse Files
                            
                            Remove
                        Upload VideoVideo uploaded successfully!Click here to viewYour Videos
                No videos uploaded yet
            
                                    Play
                                
                                    HLS
                                
                                    DASH

This page is the entry point of the application, it is built using:

  • Tailwind CSS: For fast and responsive styling
  • Alpine.js: For handling reactive state without the overhead of a full fledged framework
  • Drag and Drop File Upload: The drag-and-drop interface makes it easy for users to upload videos. When a file is dropped, the Alpine.js component updates the file state and displays the files name.
  • XHR Upload Process: The file is uploaded via an XMLHttpRequest. During the file upload, the progress is tracked and dynamically updated, providing real-time feedback to the user.
  • Video Listing: After a successful upload, the video list is updated with new entries. Each video entry includes buttons to play the video and view different streaming formats.

The implementation demonstrates careful attention to user feedback and error handling. For instance, if the upload fails, an error message is displayed to guide the user.

Now thats taken care of we can now code the player page, under templates create a new file called player.html and populate it with the following:

Video Player Back to all videosVideo Player
                        To view this video please enable JavaScript, and consider upgrading to a
                        web browser that supports HTML5 video
                    Stream URLs:HLS StreamDASH Stream

This page is designed to offer a robust video playback experience, it uses the following:

  • Video.js: A popular HTML5 video player that simplifies the integration of HLS and DASH streams.
  • HLS and DASH Sources: Both streaming formats are provided as source elements, allowing the player to choose the best method supported by the browser
  • Responsive Design: The video player is contained within a responsive container, ensuring compatibility with various screen sizes.
  • Error Handling: The player listens for error events, and any issues are logged to the console for debugging purposes.

This setup highlights how combining modern libraries and frameworks can yield a powerful, production-ready video streaming solution.

Now that the application is built, we can actually run it, first create a new requirements.txt file and populate it with the following:

flask
flask-cors
Werkzeug

Then run the following command to install the modules:

pip  requirements.txt

Then run the server with the following:

If you direct your browser to http://localhost:5000 you should see the index page, try uploading a video file and viewing it. 👀

Deployment Considerations

If you plan to deploy this application to a production environment, you will need to consider the following:

This code provides video processing synchronously, production systems should offload intensive tasks to background workers like Celery. This decouples the user experience from backend processing, ensuring the uploads are fast and responsive.

  • File Validation: Always ensure that uploaded files are validated both by type and by content.
  • Directory Permissions: The upload and output directories should have strict permissions to prevent unauthorized access.
  • CORS and CSRF Protection: Although Flask-CORS is used, further measures (like CSRF tokens) might be needed for securing the API endpoints

For high traffic, consider using a WSGI server such as Gunicorn or uWSGI behind a reverse proxy like Nginx. Additionally using cloud storage solutions for storing video files can offload file system storage from the application server.

The application uses Python's build in logging, but in production, integrate with centralized logging and monitoring systems. This ensures that errors, performance bottlenecks, and security issues are promptly identified and addressed.

In this tutorial I have shown you how to build a simple video streaming app using Python, Flask and FFmpeg.

Like my work? I post about a variety of topics, if you would like to see more please like and follow me. Also I love coffee.

items.title: How to Distribute Employee Shifts Automatically in Python
items.author: Dmitry Romanoff
items.content:
As businesses continue to grow, managing employee shifts becomes more challenging. Whether you're scheduling shifts for a team of workers or organizing a volunteer group, automation can help streamline the process. In this post, we'll look at how you can use Python to automatically assign employees to shifts with just a few lines of code.

Imagine you have a list of employees and a set of shifts that need to be assigned. It can be tedious to manually match each employee with a shift, especially when the number of employees and shifts grows. Luckily, Python can help make this task easier by distributing employees to shifts in a fair and automated manner.

In this example, we will create a Python script that distributes employees to shifts in a round-robin fashion. This means the first employee gets the first shift, the second employee gets the second shift, and so on. When we run out of employees, we start again at the first one.

Let's break down the code that accomplishes this task:

  1. :

    We begin by defining a list of employees who need to be assigned to shifts. In this case, we have seven employees: Michael, John, Linda, Jessica, Charles, Jack, and Samanta.

  2. :

    Next, we define the shifts in a list. These shifts span from Sunday to Wednesday, with various time frames. You can add as many shifts as needed depending on your requirements.

  3. Distributing Employees to Shifts:

    We then create an empty dictionary called

    to store the employee names and their corresponding shifts. The core of this logic lies in the loop:

  • We loop over each shift using . This gives us both the index () and the shift itself.
  • The line employee = employees[i % len(employees)] uses the modulus operator () to cycle through the employees. This ensures that once we reach the end of the list of employees, we start again at the first employee. For example, when , it loops back to .
  • This loop assigns an employee to each shift in a round-robin manner.
  1. Output the Shift Assignments: Finally, we loop through the dictionary and print the shift along with the employee assigned to it:

This produces output like this:

   Sun 00:00 - Sun 10:00: Michael
   Sun 10:00 - Sun 16:00: John
   Sun 16:00 - Sun 23:59: Linda
   Mon 00:00 - Mon 10:30: Jessica
   Mon 10:30 - Mon 15:59: Charles
   Mon 16:00 - Mon 20:59: Jack
   Mon 21:00 - Tue 11:59: Samanta
   Tue 12:00 - Tue 20:59: Michael
   Tue 21:00 - Tue 23:59: John
   Wed 00:00 - Wed 10:59: Linda
   Wed 11:00 - Wed 12:59: Jessica
   Wed 13:00 - Sat 23:59: Charles

Advantages of Using This Approach

  1. : This approach is incredibly simple and doesn't require complex logic. With just a few lines of code, we can achieve a fair distribution of shifts.

  2. : If you add more employees or shifts, the code will continue to work without modification. It will automatically cycle through employees based on the number of shifts.

  3. : The round-robin approach ensures that each employee gets assigned to a shift in turn, preventing any biases or overloads on specific workers.

  4. : You can easily modify the list of employees and shifts to fit your specific needs. Whether you need a larger team or different shift times, the code is flexible and scalable.

Automating shift assignments can save time and reduce errors in the workplace. Python provides a powerful and easy-to-understand way to handle this task, making it a great solution for businesses of any size. By using lists, loops, and simple logic, you can create a shift scheduling system that distributes shifts fairly and automatically.

If you’re interested in learning more about automating tasks with Python or improving your shift scheduling, be sure to check out additional Python libraries that can help, such as for managing larger datasets or for more complex shift management.

items.title: How to Automate Developer OSINT with Python
items.author: Bohdan Lukianets
items.content:
Last month, I had a surprising realization. While applying for a contract position, the client mentioned they'd already checked out several of my old Stack Overflow answers and even a GitHub issue I'd commented on two years ago. It got me thinking—just how visible is my digital footprint as a developer?

In this post, I'll show you how I built a simple Python tool leveraging Open Source Intelligence (OSINT) techniques to gather developer-related information from public sources. We'll use my own nickname "" as a practical example.

Open Source Intelligence (OSINT) refers to collecting and analyzing publicly available data. For developers, OSINT can help you quickly gather useful information such as GitHub profiles, LinkedIn profiles, open-source contributions, and professional activities.

We'll create a Python script that:

  • Accepts a developer's name or nickname.
  • Searches online sources like Google for mentions.
  • Extracts relevant LinkedIn and GitHub profile links.
  • Optionally scrapes specific websites for mentions.
  • Returns structured results for further analysis.

🛠️ Tools and Libraries Used:

  • — for sending HTTP requests.
  • — for parsing HTML content.
  • — to perform Google searches directly from Python.

Install these libraries with:

pip requests beautifulsoup4 googlesearch-python



- requests — sending HTTP requests.
- BeautifulSoup — parsing HTML content.
- googlesearch-python — to perform Google searches directly from Python.

Install these libraries with:

bash

pip install requests beautifulsoup4 googlesearch-python

⚠️ Weaknesses & Points for Improvement:
